conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I need to ask you some questions.

First, do you have any children that you're responsible for?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do. I have a child - my son Alex. He's 17 and lives with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I have a few more questions to help determine your eligibility.

Are you or your son Alex currently living in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, we both live in the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, thank you. Now I need to ask about Alex's current situation.

Is Alex currently in full-time non-advanced education or approved training? (For example, is he studying for A-levels, Scottish Highers, or similar qualifications at school or college?)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not in school or college anymore. He's actually started an apprenticeship - he's training in a trade.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I need to clarify what type of training Alex is doing.

Is Alex's apprenticeship an "approved training" programme? Specifically, is it:
- Unpaid, or
- Paid but the apprenticeship started before he turned 16?

This is important because approved training for Child Benefit purposes typically means unpaid training programmes.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Oh, it's a paid apprenticeship. He started it after he turned 16 - he's 17 now, so it wasn't that long ago.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. I need to ask one more important question to complete my assessment.

Are you the person who is primarily responsible for Alex? In other words, does Alex live with you and are you his main carer?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, absolutely. Alex lives here with me and I'm his main carer. He's my responsibility.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on the information you've provided, I'm afraid **you are not eligible for Child Benefit** for Alex.

Here's why:

While you are Alex's main carer and you both live in the UK (which are positive factors), the key issue is Alex's current situation. At 17, Alex has passed the automatic age limit of 16 for Child Benefit. 

You can continue to claim Child Benefit for children aged 16-19 **only if** they are in:
- Full-time non-advanced education (like A-levels or Scottish Highers), or
- Approved training

Alex's paid apprenticeship that started after he turned 16 does not qualify as "approved training" for Child Benefit purposes. Approved training typically refers to unpaid training programmes, or in limited cases, paid training that started before the child turned 16.

I understand this may be disappointing news, especially as Alex is still young and in training. If Alex's circumstances change in the future (for example, if he returns to full-time education), you may want to reassess your eligibility at that time.

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is fostered and the council pays for their accommodation or maintenance
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period; Child is fostered and the council pays for their accommodation or maintenance; Child has started an apprenticeship in England
circumstances: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child is fostered and the council pays for their accommodation or maintenance
Child has started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 17 years old and is not in full-time non-advanced education or approved training. He is in a paid apprenticeship that started after he turned 16. According to Child Benefit eligibility rules, you can claim for a child under 20 if they are in approved education or training. Approved training for Child Benefit purposes generally refers to unpaid training programmes, or in some cases paid training that started before the child turned 16. Since Alex's apprenticeship is paid and started after he turned 16, it does not qualify as approved training for Child Benefit purposes. Therefore, although the claimant is Alex's main carer and they both live in the UK, Child Benefit cannot be claimed for Alex in these circumstances.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is the main carer for their 17-year-old son Alex, and both live in the UK. However, Alex is not eligible for Child Benefit because he is not in full-time non-advanced education or approved training. His paid apprenticeship that started after age 16 does not qualify as approved training for Child Benefit purposes. Child Benefit typically stops when a child turns 16 unless they remain in qualifying education or training.
